title: Worm egg counts and wormer resistance
subtitle: Why routine worming damages your flock long-term, and how targeted treatment works instead.
excerpt: Blanket worming of all sheep on a fixed schedule was standard practice for decades. It is now understood to be one of the main drivers of anthelmintic resistance. Faecal egg counts take the guesswork out of treatment decisions and protect the wormers you still have.
sourceNotes: SCOPS (Sustainable Control of Parasites in Sheep), resistance management guidelines. NADIS, worm control factsheet. University of Bristol, SCOPS resistance guidance for small flocks.

glossaryTerms.definition: A heritable trait in nematode worms that allows them to survive a drug dose that would kill a susceptible worm. Resistance is selected for every time a wormer is used. Once present in a flock, it cannot be reversed. Now present to some degree in almost all UK sheep flocks for the older wormer groups.

A laboratory test on fresh sheep droppings that counts the number of worm eggs per gram of faeces. Used to decide whether treatment is necessary and to measure the effectiveness of a treatment (pre- and post-treatment FEC). Performed by many farm vets and agricultural colleges.

The population of worms in untreated animals or on pasture that has not been exposed to a wormer. Maintaining refugia is the key mechanism for slowing resistance development; susceptible worms in refugia dilute resistant survivors. Treating all animals simultaneously destroys refugia.

content.text: The principle behind current best practice is targeted selective treatment: you treat only the animals that need treatment at the time they need it, based on either a clinical sign (weight loss, scouring, bottle jaw) or a
How a faecal egg count works

Collect fresh droppings from 10 to 15 animals (or all animals in a small flock) into individual labelled bags or pooled into a group sample. The sample must be fresh, less than 24 hours old, and kept cool. Send to a vet or laboratory that offers the service. Results come back as eggs per gram (epg). Your vet will advise on the threshold above which treatment is warranted in your specific situation; this varies by age of animal, time of year, and recent treatment history.

When you do treat, check the treatment worked. Take a FEC 14 days after drenching from the same animals. If the egg count has not reduced by at least 95 per cent, the wormer group used may no longer be effective in your flock. Switch wormer groups and discuss resistance testing with your vet.

The refugia principle applies to adult ewes in normal health. Young lambs in their first grazing season have no acquired immunity and are the most vulnerable group; targeted treatment based on FEC and weight gain monitoring is still appropriate, but the treatment threshold is lower. Ewes at housing in autumn are an exception where treatment of all animals may be warranted; discuss with your vet and choose a wormer group based on a recent drench test.

Bought-in animals are a major route for introducing resistant worms. Treat all incoming animals with a wormer from a different group than you routinely use (or use a combination), house them for 2 to 3 days after treatment so any eggs passed are not deposited on your pasture, and then run a FEC before turnout. This is one of the most effective management steps a small flock holder can take.
